subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Differential Equation dy/dx = (y-1)/x² — Slope Field, Particular Solution with f(2)=0, Limit at Infinity

parts.prompt: On the axes provided, sketch a slope field for the given differential equation at the nine points indicated. (Note: Use the axes provided in the exam booklet.)
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $dy/dx = (y-1)/x^2$. At $y=1$ the slope is 0. For $y > 1$ slopes are positive; for $y < 1$ slopes are negative. Slopes are larger in magnitude near $x = 0$ than near $x = \pm 2$.

scoring_points.description: Zero slopes drawn correctly — at $y = 1$ (any $x \ne 0$), slope is $0$; the three points with $y = 1$ have horizontal segments
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: zero slopes correctly drawn — at the points where $y = 1$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{1-1}{x^2} = 0$, so horizontal slope marks.

sample_responses.response: At each of the three grid points where $y = 1$, a horizontal line segment is drawn.
sample_responses.commentary: Numerator $y - 1 = 0$ makes the slope zero at $y = 1$ regardless of $x$.

sample_responses.response: At $y = 1$, draws a slope of $1$ instead of $0$.
sample_responses.commentary: Confuses the $y$-value with the slope; when $y = 1$ the numerator $y-1 = 0$, giving slope $0$.

required_elements: Zero slopes drawn correctly — at $y = 1$ (any $x \ne 0$), slope is $0$; the three points with $y = 1$ have horizontal segments

scoring_points.description: All other slopes drawn with correct sign and relative steepness — slopes are positive where $y > 1$ and negative where $y < 1$; steeper when $|x|$ is small
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: all other slopes have correct sign ($+$ where $y > 1$, $-$ where $y < 1$) and correct relative steepness (steeper near $x = 0$ because $x^2$ is small).

sample_responses.response: Where $y > 1$: positive slopes, steeper near $x = \pm 1$ than near $x = \pm 2$. Where $y < 1$: negative slopes with the same steepness pattern.
sample_responses.commentary: Correct signs and steepness ranking: $\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{y-1}{x^2}$ is larger in magnitude when $x^2$ is smaller.

sample_responses.response: All slopes at $y = 2$ are drawn as positive but equally steep regardless of $x$.
sample_responses.commentary: Ignores the $x^2$ in the denominator; slopes at $x = 1$ are steeper than at $x = 2$ (slope is $1$ vs. $\frac{1}{4}$ for $y = 2$).

required_elements: All other slopes drawn with correct sign
relative steepness — slopes are positive where $y > 1$
negative where $y < 1$; steeper when $|x|$ is small

parts.prompt: Find the particular solution $y = f(x)$ to the differential equation with the initial condition $f(2) = 0$.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Separation gives $\ln|y-1| = -1/x + C$. Using $f(2) = 0$: $\ln 1 = -1/2 + C \Rightarrow C = 1/2$. Then $y - 1 = ke^{-1/x}$ with $k = -e^{1/2}$, so $f(x) = 1 - e^{(1/2 - 1/x)}$, $x > 0$. Max 3/6 [1-2-0-0-0] if no constant of integration. 0/6 if no separation of variables.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correctly separates variables to get $\dfrac{1}{y-1}\,dy = \dfrac{1}{x^2}\,dx$. 0/6 if no separation of variables.

sample_responses.commentary: All $y$-terms on the left, all $x$-terms on the right.
Inverts the separation; should be $\frac{1}{y-1}$ on the left and $x^{-2}$ on the right, not $(y-1)$ and $x^2$.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 2 points: correct antiderivatives — $\ln|y - 1|$ on the left and $-\dfrac{1}{x}$ on the right. Max 3/6 [1-2-0-0-0] if no constant of integration.
1 point: constant of integration $C$ present (on either side). Max 3/6 if omitted.

sample_responses.commentary: Constant of integration is present immediately after integrating.
Omits $C$; this caps the score at 3/6 per the CB rubric note.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: applies $f(2) = 0$ to find the constant. $\ln|0 - 1| = -\dfrac{1}{2} + C \Rightarrow 0 = -\dfrac{1}{2} + C \Rightarrow C = \dfrac{1}{2}$.

sample_responses.commentary: Swaps $x$ and $y$ values; $f(2) = 0$ means $x = 2$ and $y = 0$, not $x = 0$ and $y = 2$.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correctly solves for $y$ — $y - 1 = ke^{-1/x}$ with $k = -e^{1/2}$, giving $f(x) = 1 - e^{(1/2 - 1/x)}$, $x > 0$.

sample_responses.commentary: Exponentiates both sides, applies $k = -e^{1/2}$ from the initial condition, and writes the closed form.
Sign error in applying $k$; since $k = -e^{1/2} < 0$, the solution is $1 - e^{1/2 - 1/x}$, not $1 + e^{1/2 - 1/x}$.

parts.prompt: For the particular solution $y = f(x)$ described in part (b), find $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to \infty} f(x)$.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: As $x \to \infty$, $1/x \to 0$, so the exponent approaches $1/2$. $\lim_{x\to\infty} f(x) = 1 - e^{1/2} = 1 - \sqrt{e}$.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct limit $1 - e^{1/2} = 1 - \sqrt{e}$ (as $x \to \infty$, $1/x \to 0$ so the exponent approaches $1/2$).

sample_responses.commentary: Incorrectly takes the entire exponent to $0$; only $-1/x \to 0$, while the $1/2$ term remains, giving $e^{1/2}$.
